video_title: The IPO Comeback: Why Tech Giants Are Finally Going Public | All-In Liquidity IPO Panel
gist: An All-In liquidity panel with Brad Gerstner (Altimeter), Andrew Feldman (Cerebras) and Will Marshall (Planet Labs) argues the IPO window has reopened: Cerebras priced its AI-chip IPO at $185 to a ~$50-60B cap, Planet 10x'd in the public markets, and Gerstner contends the 'stay-private-forever' pendulum is swinging back, meaning more sub-$5B companies should IPO so public investors—not just private holders—capture the multi-trillion AI/space upside.

themes.theme: IPO window reopening & the stay-private pendulum

insights.claim: 2026 could be an all-time record year for IPOs, with the AI/tech listing window having swung from shut to wide open.
insights.speaker: host / panel intro
insights.so_what: Signals a re-opening primary-issuance window; watch for a wave of AI and tech listings as supply of new public equity rises.
insights.quote: Hey, 2026 could be an all-time record for IPOs.

insights.claim: Brad Gerstner argues the decade-long 'stay private forever' thesis pushed by Andreessen Horowitz is reversing, with portfolio companies now planning to IPO at $1B, $3B or $5B valuations.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner, founder/CEO Altimeter Capital
insights.so_what: If small-cap tech IPOs resume, public investors regain access to early-stage compounding; bullish for IPO underwriters and growth-equity secondary liquidity.
insights.quote: We had this period of a decade where Andrees was really pushing stay private forever and I see the pendulum swinging back

insights.claim: Gerstner rejects the view that Anthropic, OpenAI and SpaceX staying private at multi-trillion valuations is the 'new normal,' predicting public markets are shifting back toward earlier listings.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: A contrarian call against mega-private-rounds; implies the locus of AI value creation may migrate back to listed equities investors can actually buy.
insights.quote: I hear a lot of people thinking that anthropic open AI and SpaceX are the new normal. I actually think the public markets may be shifting back in this direction

insights.claim: Cerebras's path to IPO was blocked for years because the UAE was an investor, triggering CFIUS scrutiny under the Biden administration before conditions turned favorable.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Foreign (esp. Gulf) cap-table exposure is a gating risk for AI-hardware IPOs; regulatory regime change can flip a name from un-listable to hot.
insights.quote: One of their investors was the UAE. So there was questions about CPHAS

insights.claim: Gerstner frames IPO timing as binary—nine and a half years of 'really hard' followed by twelve months of 'really easy' where everybody wanted in—rather than a smoothly improving market.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: IPO windows are step-functions, not gradients; investors should position before the 'easy' 12-month window rather than chase it.
insights.quote: everything was really hard until it got really easy like n 9 and a half years of really hard and then 12 months you know of of of really easy where everybody wanted to get in

insights.claim: Feldman says Cerebras got IPO timing right only by getting it wrong for a decade, implying durable execution—not market timing—is what ultimately opens the window.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman, founder/CEO Cerebras
insights.so_what: Reinforces that survivable fundamentals, not tactical timing, gate a successful listing; a filter for evaluating IPO candidates.
insights.quote: a lot of people asked us about how we got the timing right ... the answer is by getting it wrong for a decade

insights.claim: Cerebras priced its IPO at $185 with the range raised two times, opened at ~$320, and trades around $230, implying roughly a $50-60B market cap.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: A concrete demand signal—2x upsized range and a $320 open—shows aggressive appetite for AI-silicon equity; the pullback to $230 frames a post-IPO entry band.
insights.quote: They priced the IPO at 185 which was up the range was taken up two times ... The stock opened at $320 a share I think. Today it's at 230 bucks a share, 5060 billion dollars in market cap

insights.claim: Feldman argues that going public changes nothing operational—no extra sales, no engineering progress, same vendor relationships—beyond putting more cash in the bank.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Tempers the narrative that an IPO is a business catalyst; the value is capital and validation, so post-IPO theses should rest on fundamentals not the listing event.
insights.quote: not a damn thing changes in the important parts of your business

insights.claim: Cerebras and its bankers structured an innovative 'dribble lockup' that releases shares over six months subject to performance hurdles, a structure Gerstner expects SpaceX to copy.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Performance-gated, staggered lockup releases reduce post-lock supply shocks; a structural template to watch in upcoming marquee IPOs including SpaceX.
insights.quote: you guys have innovated with the banks on what I call a dribble lockup. So over 6 months the shares can be dribbled out according to a bunch of performance hurdles

themes.theme: Where the IPO value accrues: private vs public capture

insights.claim: Gerstner contends more money is made after the IPO than before—true in both percentage and absolute terms—because venture rounds can only absorb modest capital while post-IPO markets can absorb far more.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner / Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Argues holders should not reflexively distribute at IPO; deployable capital and absolute-dollar upside favor staying long winners post-listing.
insights.quote: historically more money's made after IPO than before ... there is more money to be made both in percentage and in ... absolute

insights.claim: Altimeter invested in a company pre-IPO at a $1B valuation, distributed shares at $3-4B under LP pressure, then watched it run to $50B over the next 24 months—a cautionary example of distributing too early.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: LP-driven early distribution can forfeit the bulk of returns; a live argument for funds and LPs to hold listed winners through the lockup and beyond.
insights.quote: We invested preipo at a billion dollars. We distributed the shares I think at three or four billion and then it went to 50 billion over ... the course of the next 24 months

insights.claim: Planet Labs went public via SPAC in 2021 at a ~$2B valuation; ~90% of the value was created only in years three and four, and most early VCs (DST, Founders Fund, Capricorn) and Google held rather than sold.
insights.speaker: Will Marshall, founder/CEO Planet Labs
insights.so_what: A proof point that public-market patience captured the 10x; SPAC stigma masked a winner whose value compounded years after listing.
insights.quote: Really, it wasn't until year three or four that 90% of the value was created

insights.claim: Planet Labs' stock rose from $5 to $50 (a 10x) in the public markets over roughly 12 months, which Gerstner cites as venture-scale returns occurring on listed equity.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner / Will Marshall
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that a small-cap space name can deliver VC-like multiples publicly—supports the thesis that IPO-ing earlier hands upside to public investors.
insights.quote: over the last 12 months your stock has gone from five bucks a share to 50 bucks a share, some 10x move in the public markets

insights.claim: Gerstner argues the three multi-trillion mega-IPOs being discussed concentrate value with private-market investors, whereas earlier listings (Cerebras at ~$10B vs ~$50B, per his estimate) would have handed a ~5x to public investors.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Quantifies the cost of staying private—public investors are structurally excluded from early AI compounding; a policy/market argument for earlier IPOs.
insights.quote: Had Andrew had his way, he would have been public 18 months ago, probably at $10 billion rather than $50 billion. And that 5x ... would have gone to public market investors

insights.claim: Marshall notes most big tech companies went public at a few billion, not a few trillion, so for SpaceX to replicate that liftoff from its current valuation it would need to target 'quadrillion' valuations—an implausibly high bar.
insights.speaker: Will Marshall
insights.so_what: Flags that buying mega-cap private names near peak private valuations leaves little public upside; the math caps the multiple SpaceX investors can expect.
insights.quote: for the equivalent liftoff, SpaceX would have to be aiming at quadrillion valuations

themes.theme: Space economy: imaging, launch costs & data centers in orbit

insights.claim: Planet Labs operates the largest earth-imaging fleet (~200 satellites) imaging the entire earth daily, producing a time-series of the planet usable by farmers, energy firms, civil governments and defense.
insights.speaker: Will Marshall
insights.so_what: A unique daily-refresh geospatial dataset is the moat; recurring multi-sector demand underpins the revenue base.
insights.quote: We have the largest earth imaging fleet about 200 satellites. They image the entire earth every day.

insights.claim: Marshall estimates a $75-100B addressable market in earth observation alone, expanded by applying large language models to earth imagery.
insights.speaker: Will Marshall
insights.so_what: Sizes the near-term TAM and links it to the AI wave; an AI-data-layer angle on a listed space name.
insights.quote: I I estimate there's a 75 to$100 billion market just on Earth observation

insights.claim: Two structural cost drivers reshaped space economics: launch costs fell ~4-5x over 10 years, and satellite miniaturization cut craft that once cost ~$1B and weighed 20 tons to a few-to-hundreds of kg—analogous to the mainframe-to-desktop transition.
insights.speaker: Will Marshall
insights.so_what: The cost collapse plus miniaturization unlocks a long tail of space applications; a secular tailwind for space-data and launch-dependent businesses.
insights.quote: the same satellite that used to cost a billion dollars and weigh 20 tons now cost a few kg or a few tens or hundreds of kg

insights.claim: Planet's study with Google found orbital data centers become cheaper than terrestrial ones once launch costs fall to ~$200-300/kg; costs are ~$1,000/kg today, down ~10x in a decade, and likely to reach the threshold in 2-3 years via Starship.
insights.speaker: Will Marshall
insights.so_what: A concrete cost-curve trigger (~$200-300/kg) for the space-data-center thesis; track Starship launch pricing as the catalyst gating a trillion-dollar market.
insights.quote: when launch costs come down to about $200 to $300 a kilogram ... it would be cheaper, just simply cheaper to put the data centers in space. Now we're about $1,000 a kilogram

insights.claim: Orbital data centers can run on solar alone because a sun-synchronous dawn-dusk orbit gathers ~5x more energy per panel than ground and needs no batteries, gas or nuclear—simplifying the power problem to panels, chips and RF links.
insights.speaker: Will Marshall
insights.so_what: Reframes data centers as a power game that space solves elegantly; an argument that could redirect energy-constrained AI compute investment off-planet.
insights.quote: you can put a solar panel in a suns synchronous ... dawn dusk orbit where you're 24/7 looking at the sun. So you can have a solar panel that collects ... five times more energy per solar panel than on the ground

insights.claim: Marshall predicts that within 10 years most compute will be placed in space—a market worth trillions and larger than any other space business including earth imaging—and Planet is launching Google TPUs (after already launching Nvidia GPUs) on early tests.
insights.speaker: Will Marshall
insights.so_what: A trillion-dollar 10-year TAM call with live hardware tests; positions Planet as infrastructure for orbital compute and ties Google/Nvidia silicon to space.
insights.quote: no question within 10 years most compute will be putting in space, which to give you a sense is a lot of money, like trillions

insights.claim: Feldman is skeptical of near-term orbital data centers, noting the industry can't yet build the clusters and inter-chip communication needed even on the ground, and warns it may be a 'last 10% is 80% of the time' problem like self-driving.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: A credible technical pushback on the space-data-center timeline; tempers Planet's 10-year claim and flags clustering/interconnect as the binding constraint.
insights.quote: We're not good at doing it on the ground. We're really not good at doing it in space.

themes.theme: AI silicon: domain-specific architecture vs the GPU

insights.claim: Feldman argues AI's key economic unlock since ~2015-16 was letting computers attack images and language—previously foreclosed domains—which simultaneously expanded the addressable workload and the data being generated.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Frames AI demand as an aperture-widening of compute, not just faster chips; supports a durable, broad-based compute-consumption growth thesis.
insights.quote: what AI did starting in about 2015 16 is it opened the door the aperture

insights.claim: Feldman's framework: new workloads are when compute market share changes hands—graphics birthed Nvidia, mobile moved share to ARM from Intel/AMD, networking created Cisco/Juniper/Arista—and AI is the next such shift.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: A reusable lens: workload transitions, not incumbency, redistribute silicon share—justifies betting on AI-native challengers over incumbents.
insights.quote: Share changed when the rise of graphics emerged and you got the dedicated GPU. That's how Nvidia was born.

insights.claim: Feldman argues that to be 20x better than Nvidia an architecture cannot look like a GPU, because a GPU-shaped design has no path to beat the incumbent who already harvested the low-hanging fruit.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: A differentiation test for evaluating AI-chip challengers—me-too GPU designs are dismissible; only radically different architectures can take share.
insights.quote: if you build a GPU, the odds that you're better than Nvidia in our view are approximately zero

insights.claim: Cerebras's bet was a dinner-plate-sized wafer chip with memory placed next to compute, solving the core memory-to-compute data-movement bottleneck and enabling faster memory than postage-stamp-sized GPUs allow.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Names the architectural moat (wafer-scale + co-located memory) behind Cerebras's speed claims; the technical basis for its differentiation story.
insights.quote: The hard part here ... is moving data from memory to compute. This is the fundamental problem in AI. And we solved it ... to build a very big chip and to put memory right next to compute.

insights.claim: Feldman claims Cerebras runs 15-18x faster than a GPU when OpenAI uses it, arguing inference latency is existential because users won't wait for AI any more than they tolerate dial-up or slow search.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Positions latency as the competitive axis in inference and names OpenAI as a customer—an edge that could drive share gains in real-time AI workloads.
insights.quote: when OpenAI uses us, we're 15 or 18 times faster than a GPU

insights.claim: Gerstner argues the scrutiny of public markets sharpens focus and accelerates innovation—'steel sharpens steel'—so going public sooner is where great companies are forged, not just where liquidity is found.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Reframes the IPO as an operational catalyst via accountability; supports a structural bullish case for the renewed early-IPO trend.
insights.quote: getting public sooner having the scrutiny of public markets ... sharpens the focus it steel sharpens steel iron sharpens iron

insights.claim: Marshall predicts AI plus space converge into 'large earth models'/'planetary intelligence'—feeding real-world satellite data into LLMs that are otherwise 'blind' to the physical world—unlocking a huge new application economy.
insights.speaker: Will Marshall
insights.so_what: Positions proprietary earth data as the scarce input that grounds LLMs in reality; a differentiated AI-data thesis distinct from text-only models.
insights.quote: all the cool stuff that we're doing on the with LLMs now is really based on just the text of the internet ... they don't know about the real world. I call them blind

insights.claim: Marshall reframes Planet from a passive map/data source toward a tool for accomplishing tasks—including security/defense use cases that now form a larger revenue fraction than originally expected—by giving customers advance warning to prevent conflict.
insights.speaker: Will Marshall
insights.so_what: A defense-revenue mix shift raises both growth and geopolitical/regulatory exposure; relevant to valuing Planet as part dual-use defense-tech.
insights.quote: security ... is part of the initial thing that we said we would do ... but it's true there's a bigger fraction today than perhaps we would have guessed
